summary: Mary Wollstonecraft was an 18th-century English writer and philosopher who advocated for women's educational and social equality. In her seminal work, A Vindication of the Rights of Woman, she argued that the contemporary educational system deliberately made women frivolous, and that equal education would benefit all of society by producing capable workers, wives, and mothers.

principles.statement: Women and men must be educated equally.
principles.rationale: Allowing girls the same educational advantages as boys results in women who are not only exceptional wives and mothers but also capable workers in many professions, which benefits all of society.

principles.statement: The betterment of women's status must be achieved through political change and the radical reform of national educational systems.
principles.rationale: Systemic political reform is required to dismantle the deliberate training of women to be incapable, moving beyond mere pleas for improved education.

anti_patterns.description: Educational systems that deliberately train women to be frivolous and incapable.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It denies women the same advantages as boys, preventing them from becoming capable professionals and exceptional mothers, ultimately depriving society of their full potential.
